title: Barts Comet

jokes.text: our most propane-explosion-free Science Week ever
jokes.setup: Science Week should be impressive and educational
jokes.punchline: The best they can say is they didn't blow anything up
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons bureaucratic incompetence - the low bar of success is darkly funny

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Science Week should be impressive and educational.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: The best they can say is they didn't blow anything up.

jokes.text: I can't say I approve of the 'whoo,' but the 'hurrah' was quite heartening
jokes.setup: Kids cheer enthusiastically for science
jokes.punchline: Skinner analyzes the cheering with bureaucratic precision
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner character moment - turning enthusiasm into administrative analysis shows his rigid mindset

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, that's a lovely display of enthusiasm, but I'm afraid we need to file the proper forms before any cheering can be officially sanctioned. There are noise ordinances to consider, and we'll need signed parental consent for decibel levels above 85.

jokes.text: Bart, if you have a failing, it's that you're always demanding perfection. If you have a failing.
jokes.setup: Bart drew an unflattering caricature of Skinner on the weather balloon
jokes.punchline: Skinner sarcastically calls Bart a perfectionist while passive-aggressively insulting him
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Skinner's passive-aggressive nature, though not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. A perfectionist, I see. Only you could capture the exact proportions of my chin and the precise angle of my disappointment.

jokes.text: Careful, children. That's my car.
jokes.setup: Children are climbing on something to reach the balloon
jokes.punchline: Skinner's priority is protecting his car rather than the children's safety
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical character beat revealing Skinner's misplaced priorities

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: No! Not the car! Watch the car! The car!

jokes.text: Tango 14, we're being fired at. I'm getting an ID on the bogey now... Iraqis again. Launching Sidewinder missile.
jokes.setup: Military response to a weather balloon
jokes.punchline: Treating a school science project like enemy aircraft
jokes.explanation: Dark satire of military overreach, though the Iraq reference feels dated

dialogue.speaker: Military Pilot
dialogue.line: We have an unidentified flying object. Weapons locked. Ready to fire.

dialogue.speaker: Military Commander
dialogue.line: Hold your fire! That's just a weather balloon.

dialogue.speaker: Military Pilot
dialogue.line: Sir, it's heading toward the school. It could be a science project.

dialogue.speaker: Military Commander
dialogue.line: A science project? Fire at will!

jokes.text: This is what happens when you cut money out of the military and put it into healthcare. - It's a good program. Just give it a chance, that's all I ask.
jokes.setup: Military missed their target twice
jokes.punchline: Blames healthcare funding for military incompetence
jokes.explanation: Political satire that's competent but not particularly sharp or memorable

dialogue.speaker: Military Pilot
dialogue.line: We've missed our target twice.

dialogue.speaker: Military Pilot
dialogue.line: It's the healthcare funding. We can't afford better pilots.

jokes.text: Curse the man who invented helium. Curse Pierre Jules César Janssen.
jokes.setup: Skinner is frustrated with the balloon situation
jokes.punchline: He curses helium's discoverer with surprising specificity
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from general frustration to specific scientific knowledge - very Skinner-esque

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: A pox upon you, Sir William Ramsay, Scottish chemist and 1904 Nobel laureate!

jokes.text: Empty my pockets, you say? Well, certainly, but I fail to see how...
jokes.setup: Skinner asks Bart to empty his pockets to find evidence
jokes.punchline: Bart feigns innocence while obviously guilty
jokes.explanation: Standard Bart mischief - well-executed but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Bart, empty your pockets. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Principal Skinner, I don't know what you're talking about. I'm completely innocent.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Is that so?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba!

jokes.text: Blueprints of the dummy, notarized photos of you making the dummy, and an alternate wording for the banner.
jokes.setup: Skinner found evidence in Bart's pockets
jokes.punchline: Bart had ridiculously thorough documentation of his prank
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing Bart's unexpected thoroughness in planning pranks

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Bart, I found these items in your pockets. Care to explain?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, sir, those are just some things I was carrying around.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Just things? This appears to be a detailed schematic, a timeline, signed witness statements, and a cost-benefit analysis!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I like to be thorough with my pranks, sir.

jokes.text: There's a 4:30 in the morning now?
jokes.setup: Skinner says they'll start at 4:30 AM
jokes.punchline: Bart is shocked this time exists
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart line - kid logic that's universally relatable and well-delivered

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: We'll start at 4:30 AM.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: 4:30 AM? There's a 4:30 in the morning?

jokes.text: Top of the hour, time for the news. But of course, there is no news yet. Everyone's still asleep in their comfy, comfy beds. Good night, everybody.
jokes.setup: It's 4:30 AM news time
jokes.punchline: Nothing happens this early, so there's no news to report
jokes.explanation: Nice observational humor about early morning emptiness, though not deeply memorable

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: It's 4:30 AM news time. Nothing happens this early, so there's no news to report.

jokes.text: There's a stuffed pepper in the trash from last night. Just rinse it good.
jokes.setup: Bart asks for breakfast at 4:30 AM
jokes.punchline: Marge suggests garbage food as a reasonable option
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor showing the family's low standards, though not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, can I have breakfast?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, it's 4:30 in the morning.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose you could have some of that garbage food from last night.

jokes.text: Come on, Lassie. Here, boy. Come on, girl. Attaboy.
jokes.setup: Bart is calling for what seems to be the family dog
jokes.punchline: He doesn't know the dog's gender and just keeps switching
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment showing Bart's confusion or indifference to pet details

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Here boy! Come on, girl! Here boy! Come on, girl! Here boy! Come on, girl!

jokes.text: There's nothing more exciting than science. You get all the fun of sitting still, being quiet, writing down numbers, paying attention. Science has it all.
jokes.setup: Skinner is trying to make science sound exciting to Bart
jokes.punchline: He lists the most boring possible activities as 'fun'
jokes.explanation: Excellent Skinner moment - perfectly captures his tone-deaf enthusiasm and bureaucratic mindset

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Science is all around us, Simpson. It's exciting! Why, just yesterday we conducted an experiment on the coefficient of friction.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Sounds like a blast.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: It was! We also calculated the trajectory of a falling object, observed the crystalline structure of salt, and measured the pH levels of various household substances.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Principal Skinner? That sounds like the opposite of fun.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Fun? It's not about fun, Simpson. It's about understanding the fundamental principles of the universe through rigorous mathematical analysis and meticulous record-keeping.

jokes.text: Why don't they look anything like their names?
jokes.setup: Skinner points out constellations
jokes.punchline: Bart asks the question everyone thinks about star patterns
jokes.explanation: Universal observational humor that many people can relate to

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: And there's Orion, the hunter. And there's his dog, Canis Major.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So like, who connects all the dots? Is there a guy up there doing it, or did God just naturally make them that way?

jokes.text: Once. But by the time I got to a phone, my discovery had already been reported by Principal Kohoutek.
jokes.setup: Skinner talks about hoping to discover something
jokes.punchline: His one discovery was stolen by another principal
jokes.explanation: Good character backstory showing Skinner's petty rivalries and failures

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: I've always hoped to discover something that would change education forever.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, I did discover something once.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: But Principal Superintendent took all the credit for it.

jokes.text: I got back at him, though. Him and that little boy of his.
jokes.setup: Skinner mentions getting revenge on Principal Kohoutek
jokes.punchline: He also got revenge on the principal's child for some reason
jokes.explanation: Dark and unexpected - shows Skinner's vindictive nature extending to innocent children

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: He also got revenge on the principal's child for some reason

jokes.text: No sighting. Did you get that one, Bart? - Hell, no. - Good.
jokes.setup: Skinner asks if Bart recorded the data
jokes.punchline: Bart admits he didn't, and Skinner approves
jokes.explanation: Nice subversion - Skinner is actually fine with Bart not doing the work

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Did you record the data?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No, I didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Excellent work, Bart.

jokes.text: Observatory? This is Bart Simpson. I see something in the sky at 4-12-8, and the last number is seven.
jokes.setup: Bart calls the observatory about his discovery
jokes.punchline: He doesn't know the proper coordinates system
jokes.explanation: Good kid logic - Bart tries to be scientific but doesn't understand the system

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, hi, I'm calling about this thing I discovered in the sky.

dialogue.speaker: Observatory Worker
dialogue.line: What are the coordinates?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, it's right there, above that big tree, next to the moon.

jokes.text: Got you, my rumpy doppelgänger.
jokes.setup: Skinner is looking through the telescope
jokes.punchline: He uses an odd phrase combining 'rumpy' with 'doppelgänger'
jokes.explanation: Bizarre Skinner vocabulary - 'rumpy doppelgänger' is memorably weird

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Good heavens! I've discovered a rumpy doppelgänger!

jokes.text: And then I sped away without anyone seeing my license plate.
jokes.setup: Homer is telling about his day
jokes.punchline: He casually mentions fleeing from some incident
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer casual criminality - treats hit-and-run as mundane

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So I'm telling you, I had to flee from this incident at work today.

jokes.text: Sounds like you had a good day, Dad. - Yeah. Except I forgot to go to work.
jokes.setup: Lisa responds to Homer's story positively
jokes.punchline: Homer forgot his most basic responsibility
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer incompetence - treats major failure as minor detail

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's wonderful, Dad!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know. I'm a great father.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You forgot Maggie at the store.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Oh, honey, I'm so proud of you. But then you've always been proud of me. - Yes.
jokes.setup: Marge expresses pride in Bart's comet discovery
jokes.punchline: Bart incorrectly assumes she's always been proud, Marge reluctantly agrees
jokes.explanation: Good family dynamic humor - Marge caught between honesty and kindness

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh Bart, I'm so proud of you for discovering that comet!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, you've always been proud of me?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well... I suppose so, yes.

jokes.text: I am called Ham, since I enjoy Ham Radio. This is E-Mail, Cosine, Report Card, Database and Lisa.
jokes.setup: Introducing the Super Friends club members
jokes.punchline: All have ridiculous nicknames based on nerdy interests, except Lisa
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor with the contrast that Lisa gets no special nickname

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Welcome to the Super Friends club. We have Aquaman, Superman, Wonder Woman... and then there's Ham.

dialogue.speaker: Ham
dialogue.line: Actually, my superhero name is Captain Commodore.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Right, because you're obsessed with retro computing. The rest of you have ridiculous nicknames based on nerdy interests too.

dialogue.speaker: Nerd 1
dialogue.line: I'm Byte-Master, named after my favorite programming language!

dialogue.speaker: Nerd 2
dialogue.line: And I'm Dungeonmaster Supreme!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Except for me. I'm just... Lisa.

jokes.text: This is E-Mail, Cosine, Report Card, Database and Lisa.
jokes.setup: Introducing the Super Friends group
jokes.punchline: All have tech/academic nicknames except Lisa who uses her real name
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of nerd naming convention with Lisa as the normal one among the group

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm Lisa Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm Bartman.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm Radiation Boy.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: I'm Fallout Boy.

jokes.text: I make it a point never turn my head unless I expect to see something.
jokes.setup: Bart points out his comet in the sky
jokes.punchline: Database has an absurdly rigid rule about looking around
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd character trait - extreme nerd efficiency taken to ridiculous extreme

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey, check it out! There's a comet!

dialogue.speaker: Database
dialogue.line: According to my calculations, the proper way to observe a comet is to stand perfectly still, face due north, and blink exactly 47 times. Any deviation from this protocol will result in improper comet observation.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, it's just a comet. You can look at it however you want.

dialogue.speaker: Database
dialogue.line: Incorrect. I have established protocols, Simpson. Your casual approach to celestial observation is precisely why society is crumbling.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no! This isn't right at all! It must be coming toward us at a fantastic speed.
jokes.setup: Lisa looks at Bart's comet through the telescope
jokes.punchline: She immediately realizes the danger that everyone else missed
jokes.explanation: Good plot advancement that's true to Lisa's character as the smart one

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: My God! It's headed straight for us! We only have 73 days!

jokes.text: I knew you'd try to find something wrong with my comet. You've always been petty and small, right from the beginning.
jokes.setup: Lisa points out the comet will kill everyone
jokes.punchline: Bart treats this as typical sibling rivalry
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart response - he's so self-centered he thinks the apocalypse is about him

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, the comet will kill everyone.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh sure, Lisa. And I suppose you'll live?

jokes.text: We are the Super Friends - Hey, shut up.
jokes.setup: The Super Friends are singing their theme song while rushing to help
jokes.punchline: Someone tells them to shut up mid-song
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of heroic moment - nerds get reality check

dialogue.speaker: Super Friends
dialogue.line: 🎵 Super Friends, Super Friends, we're on our way to help you! 🎵

dialogue.speaker: Random person
dialogue.line: Shut up!

dialogue.speaker: Super Friends
dialogue.line: 🎵 Super Friends, Super Friends— 🎵

jokes.text: Sounds like the Doomsday Whistle. Ain't been blown for nigh on to three years. Trouble a-brewing.
jokes.setup: Emergency whistle blows
jokes.punchline: Moe knows about a 'Doomsday Whistle' like it's routine
jokes.explanation: Great Springfield lore - implies doomsday is a regular enough occurrence to have protocols

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Doomsday Whistle? Yeah, I know about that. It goes off every third Thursday at noon. Half the time I think it's a tornado, the other half I think it's just Barney setting off the car alarm again.

jokes.text: when I learned about the impending crisis... I caught the very next plane to Springfeld. Field.
jokes.setup: Official claims to have rushed to help Springfield
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces the town name despite claiming urgency to get there
jokes.explanation: Political satire about incompetent officials who don't even know where they're supposed to help

dialogue.speaker: Government official
dialogue.line: I rushed right over here as fast as I could to help!

dialogue.speaker: Government official
dialogue.line: How can I assist you people of... Sprinch-field?

jokes.text: when I learned about the impending crisis, I caught the very next plane to Springfeld. Field.
jokes.setup: Official announces he came to help Springfield
jokes.punchline: He mispronounces Springfield and corrects himself awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Minor wordplay gag - politician can't even get the town name right

dialogue.speaker: Government Official
dialogue.line: I came here to help Springfieldia... I mean Springfield.

jokes.text: You don't need to applaud that.
jokes.setup: Official announces the comet will hit Springfield
jokes.punchline: People apparently applauded the bad news
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing Springfield citizens' confusion about appropriate responses

dialogue.speaker: Government Official
dialogue.line: The comet will hit Springfield.

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: [applause]

jokes.text: working with former Carter-administration officials and military men who were forced into early retirement... For various reasons which we won't go into here, okay?
jokes.setup: Frink explains the team assembled to help
jokes.punchline: The 'experts' are all disgraced failures
jokes.explanation: Good dark satire about incompetent government response teams

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: I've assembled a team of experts to help us with this problem.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: We have a former astronaut, a disbarred lawyer, and a man who was fired from the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: In other words, the finest minds money can't buy.

jokes.text: The reason we have elected officials is so we don't have to think all the time.
jokes.setup: Lisa expresses concern about the comet plan
jokes.punchline: Homer explains his philosophy of democratic laziness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer political philosophy - perfectly captures American civic disengagement with comic precision

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look, Lisa, in this country we have something called democracy. And it's a beautiful thing. We vote on stuff, and if it passes, we just sit back and complain about it. That's the American way!

jokes.text: Just like that rain-forest scare a few years back. Our officials saw there was a problem, and they fixed it, didn't they? - No, Dad. I don't think... - There's that word again.
jokes.setup: Homer cites rainforest as example of government success
jokes.punchline: Lisa tries to correct him but he cuts her off for 'thinking'
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to Homer's earlier line, showing his consistent anti-intellectual stance

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You see, the rainforest is a perfect example of government working.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, Dad, the rainforest isn't a government—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, Lisa, Lisa... quiet. You're thinking again.

jokes.text: See, while the unprepared are still sitting around twiddling their thumbs and going: [Homer makes confused noises]
jokes.setup: Homer explains his backup plan
jokes.punchline: He demonstrates confused people with silly sound effects
jokes.explanation: Physical Homer humor that's more visual than dialogue-based

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, if my backup plan doesn't work out, I'll just make silly sound effects and confuse people.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Boing! Sproing! Woop woop woop woop!

jokes.text: It blew up the bridge! We're doomed!
jokes.setup: The rocket was supposed to save them
jokes.punchline: It destroys their escape route instead
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing government incompetence making things worse

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The rocket was supposed to save us!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It destroyed our escape route instead.

jokes.text: And like Icarus, the rocket foolishly soared too high and lost control of its servo-guidance mechanism
jokes.setup: News anchor explains what happened to the rocket
jokes.punchline: Mixes classical mythology with technical jargon
jokes.explanation: Nice contrast between poetic language and technical failure

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: In a stunning turn of events, the rocket has achieved what scientists call 'escape velocity,' or as the ancients knew it, 'Hermes doing his thing.' The vessel, much like Icarus flying too close to the sun, has instead flown too close to... well, the sun. Experts confirm the spacecraft has reached what they're calling 'a really bad trajectory,' which is Latin for 'we're all doomed.'

jokes.text: It's a silent testament to the never-give-up and never-think-things-out spirit of our citizens.
jokes.setup: Citizens are jumping the gorge with cars
jokes.punchline: Reporter describes recklessness as positive spirit
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the anti-thinking theme while describing Springfield's general stupidity

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: What we're witnessing here is a reckless disregard for human life... which some might call the American spirit!

jokes.text: I wanna tack on a rider to that bill. Thirty million dollars of taxpayer money to support the perverted arts.
jokes.setup: Congress is about to pass Springfield evacuation bill
jokes.punchline: Congressman adds unrelated arts funding to disaster relief
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire about congressional dysfunction and partisan priorities

dialogue.speaker: Congressman
dialogue.line: I move to add $50 million in federal funding for the arts to this disaster relief bill.

dialogue.speaker: Other Congressman
dialogue.line: But sir, this is an evacuation bill for Springfield!

dialogue.speaker: Congressman
dialogue.line: I know what I'm doing. All in favor?

jokes.text: I've said it before and I'll say it again, democracy simply doesn't work.
jokes.setup: Congress fails to pass the evacuation bill
jokes.punchline: News anchor casually dismisses entire democratic system
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons dark humor - casual dismissal of democracy with perfect deadpan delivery

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Well, it looks like Congress has failed to pass the evacuation bill. But don't worry folks, I'm sure they'll get around to it... eventually. In the meantime, why not try our new Democracy-flavored soda? It tastes just like broken promises and disappointment.

jokes.text: The following people are gay. - Turn it off. - Just a second.
jokes.setup: Brockman says he'll reveal secrets before the world ends
jokes.punchline: Homer wants to hear the gossip despite the situation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's priorities and Simpsons' boundary-pushing humor

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose if the world's ending, there's no point in keeping secrets anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ooh! Ooh! Tell us! Tell us!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, shouldn't we be more concerned about, you know, the world ending?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, when am I ever going to get another chance to hear juicy gossip from a news anchor?

jokes.text: If I'm not, may we all be horribly crushed from above somehow.
jokes.setup: Homer expresses confidence about the comet
jokes.punchline: Makes a curse that exactly describes their current situation
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup for the comet threat with Homer's oblivious curse

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry. I'm sure it'll burn up in the atmosphere and everything will be fine.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: May the Lord strike me down if I'm wrong!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: It'll burn up in our atmosphere and what's ever left will be no bigger than a Chihuahua's head.
jokes.setup: Homer dismisses the comet threat
jokes.punchline: Uses oddly specific and random comparison for size
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer random comparison - 'Chihuahua's head' is memorably weird and specific

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A comet? That's not a threat, that's just a big space rock. It's probably no bigger than... than a really big football stadium made of cheese!

jokes.text: We have a bomb shelter? - Homer Simpson takes care of his family.
jokes.setup: Family discovers they have a bomb shelter
jokes.punchline: Homer takes credit despite them not knowing about it
jokes.explanation: Good Homer ego moment - takes credit for accidental preparedness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, well, well... looks like my bomb shelter saved the day! Who's the genius now?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we didn't even know we had a bomb shelter.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! That's how good I am at keeping secrets. You're welcome.

jokes.text: Hidy-ho-a-roonie, neighbor. What can I do you for? - Get out of there. My family needs your bomb shelter.
jokes.setup: Homer demands access to Ned's shelter
jokes.punchline: Homer's blunt selfishness vs. Ned's cheerful politeness
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer-Ned dynamic - selfishness meets kindness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ned, I need to use your bomb shelter. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, gosh, Homer, I'd love to help, but it's not really a good time. We're having a little get-together with the—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't care about your stupid party. My family needs that shelter.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Gee willikers, Homer, I understand you're upset, but maybe we could work out a compromise. How about you come back tomorrow and—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Tomorrow? Are you out of your mind? I need it now! What if something happens in the next five minutes?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, the odds of that are pretty slim, neighbor. But if you'd like, you could always—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know what? Forget it. You're a terrible Christian, Ned.

jokes.text: We haven't got shelterinies. We want in yours. - Well, sorry. Shelter's kind of full.
jokes.setup: People want into Ned's shelter
jokes.punchline: They adopt Ned's made-up cute words, he reluctantly refuses
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Ned's speech patterns and shows his breaking point

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, howdy-diddly-do, folks! Welcome to my shelter!

dialogue.speaker: Random person
dialogue.line: This place is great! Hey, what do you call those cute little words you use?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Oh, those are just my diddly-doodly expressions of Christian fellowship!

dialogue.speaker: Random person
dialogue.line: Can we use them too? That's so neat-a-rooney!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I... I suppose you could... but please don't overdo the diddly-daddly—

dialogue.speaker: Random person
dialogue.line: This food is flavorino! You're such a good-diddly-good person, Neddy!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Now look here, that's quite enough of the cutesy-wutesy talk! Those words are mine!

jokes.text: In the world of the future, we'll need laughter, so I'm in.
jokes.setup: Group decides who stays in the shelter
jokes.punchline: Krusty argues for his own importance
jokes.explanation: Standard character moment - Krusty's self-serving argument

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey, hey! You can't kick me out! I'm a celebrity! I got fans!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Krusty, there's only five people in this shelter.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: So? I'm still more important than most of you!

jokes.text: We all know the one thing we won't need in the future: Left-handed stores. That's you, Flanders!
jokes.setup: Group is deciding who to kick out of shelter
jokes.punchline: Homer targets Ned with absurd logic about left-handed stores
jokes.explanation: Great Homer logic and character dynamic - absurd reasoning to target his neighbor

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We gotta kick somebody out. I vote for the Flanders.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why would you kick out Ned?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Because he's left-handed. And left-handed stores are a scam.

jokes.text: I might go mad with fear out there, so, Todd, I want you to shoot Daddy if he tries to get back in. - Okay, Dad.
jokes.setup: Ned is being kicked out of his own shelter
jokes.punchline: He instructs his young son to potentially kill him
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark moment - Ned's extreme selflessness becomes disturbing

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, if it comes to that, Todd, you know what you have to do.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Flanders
dialogue.line: Yes, Dad.

jokes.text: I'll make the sound of a barnyard animal, and you all try to guess what it is.
jokes.setup: Moe suggests a game while waiting for death
jokes.punchline: Chooses the most childish possible activity for adults in crisis
jokes.explanation: Character humor showing Moe's simple entertainment suggestions for apocalypse situation

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Hey, while we're waitin' for death, you wanna play a game?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I spy with my little eye... something beginning with 'D'.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: D... for Death!

jokes.text: I'll make the sound of a barnyard animal, and you all try to guess what it is.
jokes.setup: Group is trapped and tense in the shelter
jokes.punchline: Moe suggests a childish game to pass time
jokes.explanation: Sets up the coming chaos with Moe's misguided attempt at entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Hey, I got an idea. Let's play a game to pass the time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What kind of game?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I Spy.

jokes.text: Can't you see this barnyard-noise guessing game is tearing us apart?
jokes.setup: The animal guessing game has caused fighting
jokes.punchline: Lisa dramatically blames a silly game for their breakdown
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - absurd that such a simple game causes complete breakdown

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is all because of that ridiculous guessing game! It's torn this family apart! A silly parlor game has destroyed everything we hold dear!

jokes.text: Can't you see this barnyard-noise guessing game... is tearing us apart?
jokes.setup: Minor disagreement about animal sounds
jokes.punchline: Homer frames trivial game as destroying the group
jokes.explanation: Good escalation humor with Homer's dramatic overreaction to minor conflict

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I'm gonna play a little game with you guys.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What kind of game?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's called 'Who Can Keep Quiet the Longest.' Whoever makes a noise loses and I win.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And if I win, I get to destroy this family.

jokes.text: I can't let that brave man out there die alone. I'm surprised and disgusted by all of you. Especially his children.
jokes.setup: Homer decides to leave the shelter to join Ned
jokes.punchline: Homer acts morally superior while insulting Ned's kids
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's confused moral compass

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm outta here, Flanders. I'm going back to my family where I belong.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, bless your heart, Homer. We'll miss you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, at least my kids aren't a bunch of goody-two-shoes wimps like yours.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Homer!

jokes.text: Radio reception in there sucked.
jokes.setup: Everyone leaves the shelter to face death together
jokes.punchline: Barney reveals his mundane reason for leaving
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of noble moment with Barney's practical concern

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: I'm leaving because I left my car keys in here somewhere and I'm not gonna lose another set to these people.

jokes.text: Let's go burn down the observatory so this'll never happen again.
jokes.setup: Everyone is relieved the comet burned up
jokes.punchline: Moe suggests destroying the observatory to prevent future discoveries
jokes.explanation: Brilliant anti-intellectual mob logic - destroy science to prevent scary discoveries

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: You know, we should destroy that observatory so it can never discover another comet again.

jokes.text: I can't believe that extra-thick layer of pollution that I've picketed against is what burned up the comet.
jokes.setup: The comet was destroyed by atmospheric pollution
jokes.punchline: Lisa's environmental activism accidentally saved everyone
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic twist - pollution as savior subverts environmental messaging

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: My environmental activism accidentally saved everyone.

jokes.text: Yeah. Dad was right. - I know, kids. I'm scared too.
jokes.setup: Lisa acknowledges Homer was right about the comet
jokes.punchline: Homer misunderstands and thinks they're scared of his being right
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending moment - Homer's misunderstanding caps his accidental wisdom perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, you were right about the comet.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I was right! I was right!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We're all going to die.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

tmdb_overview: When Bart is forced to assist Principal Skinner in early-morning astronomy observations, he discovers a giant comet heading towards Springfield. Facing impending doom, the entire town shows up at the Flanders family bomb shelter.
insight: Comet panic triggers 60 jokes in 22 minutes—a Simpsons density peak.
